results.possibleIdentity: Robert Elliot Pollack (born September 2, 1940) is an American biologist and researcher who was active at New York University Medical School in the late 1960s. Born and raised in Brooklyn, New York, he earned a BA in physics from Columbia University in 1961 and a PhD in biological sciences from Brandeis University in 1966. Between 1966 and 1969, he served as a postdoctoral fellow and instructor in the Department of Pathology at NYU Medical Center (now NYU Langone Health). During this period, he conducted research on mammalian cell culture and the reversion of cancer cells, working alongside notable scientists such as Howard Green and Mary Weiss. His technical expertise spans medicine, biology, and the emerging field of bio-engineering (specifically genetic engineering and the ethics of recombinant DNA). Pollack is well known for his later career at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory and as a long-time Professor of Biological Sciences and Dean of Columbia College. He has a significant interest in the intersection of science, art, and the humanities; his wife, Amy Pollack, is an artist with whom he has collaborated on various projects and books, such as 'The Course of Nature'. This cross-disciplinary focus aligns with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a service organization for collaborations between artists and technical experts. Although primarily a biologist, his E.A.T. association likely involved providing technical guidance to artists in fields like medicine, biology, and optics (potentially holography) during his tenure at NYU and beyond.
